Mumbai rains: Quick commerce, food delivery firms activate monsoon playbook as red alert tests operations
Food delivery and quick commerce platforms have activated their annual monsoon preparedness measures as Mumbai remains under a red alert for heavy rainfall, with companies saying there has been no immediate impact on operations so far.
People familiar with the matter told Moneycontrol that order volumes and deliveries have remained largely stable despite the heavy rains, although they cautioned that it was still early in the weather event and that operational measures could be stepped up if conditions worsen.
"We haven't seen any significant impact yet. It's still early, but we're monitoring the situation closely," an executive at a leading quick commerce firm said.
As part of their routine monsoon playbooks, platforms have begun rolling out measures aimed at safeguarding delivery partners while maintaining service continuity. These include distributing raincoats and other protective gear to riders, stocking first-aid kits at dark stores and conducting awareness drives that encourage delivery partners to avoid prolonged exposure to rain and seek assistance if required.
The preparations extend beyond rider safety. According to another person at a quick commerce company, technology also plays a key role in helping platforms respond to changing weather conditions in real time.
"Delivery radiuses can get shorter if the rains intensify. Some areas may temporarily become unserviceable because of flooding or waterlogging, but our forecasting systems help us actively manage that by diverting riders to areas with higher demand and adjusting operations accordingly," the person said.
People cited above said such interventions form part of standard operating procedures during the monsoon and are intended to help platforms respond to localised disruptions while minimising the impact on the broader delivery network.
Decisions around delivery radiuses, rider deployment and serviceability are reviewed continuously based on factors such as rainfall intensity, road accessibility and demand patterns.
"Our teams are preparing for the rains so that our delivery partners stay safe and we can continue serving customers in need as much as possible," said an Eternal spokesperson.
Swiggy and Zepto did not respond to Moneycontrol's queries till the time of publishing.
Industry executives said demand for groceries and other essentials could rise during spells of heavy rainfall as consumers choose to stay indoors, though they cautioned that it was too early to draw conclusions about demand trends or the broader operational impact of the ongoing rains.
For now, companies are continuing to monitor weather conditions and could make further operational adjustments, including modifying delivery radiuses or temporarily limiting services in severely affected localities, if required, while prioritising the safety of delivery partners.
